info.name: Olympus Mobile Server - Super Admin APIs
info.description:
Super-admin (Admin Panel) API surface of the Olympus Mobile Server.

Every endpoint the admin panel calls, grouped by admin screen. Requests are ordered so a folder can be
run top to bottom — where an object has a lifecycle (users, mount points, AD/LDAP settings, embedding
capabilities) the folder runs **create → read → update → delete**, and the create step stores the new id
in a collection variable that the later requests consume.

## Getting started

1. Import the companion environment (`Olympus Super Admin - example.postman_environment.json`) or edit
   this collection's variables directly.
2. Set `baseUrl` to your API origin. **This is the `mobile-api.*` host, not the app host** — e.g.
   `https://mobile-api.your-company.com`. The app itself is served from a different origin on purpose,
   so that user-uploaded content can never read a token out of the app origin's `localStorage`.
3. Set `clientApiKey` to an active App Client key. The browser app reads it from
   `<appUrl>/assets/data/config/appConfig.json`.
4. Set `adminEmail` / `adminPassword`, then run **00 · Authentication → Login as Super Admin**. Its test
   script stores `adminToken` and `refreshToken`; collection-level Bearer auth then covers every other
   request.

## Auth model

The collection sets `Authorization: Bearer {{adminToken}}` at collection level. Requests that bypass the
`is-logged-in` policy (`entrance/*`, `token/*`, the public onboarding endpoints) are individually marked
**No Auth**.

Access tokens are short-lived — when requests start returning `401 BAD_TOKEN`, run
**POST /api/v1/token/refresh-access-token** (it refreshes `adminToken` in place) or log in again.

Almost everything here is gated by the `is-super-admin` policy. Where an endpoint sits at a different
tier — `is-logged-in`, public, or `is-super-admin` **plus** an Enterprise licence — the request
description says so explicitly.

## Response envelope

Most actions answer `{ "success": true, "data": { ... } }` and errors answer
`{ "errors": [ { "code", "message" } ] }`. Proxied GenAI endpoints and a few embedding-admin actions
return bare JSON instead, and the CSV exports return `text/csv` — use *Send and Download* for those.

## About the examples

Saved example responses were captured against a live Olympus install, including the real error shapes
(`400 BAD_INPUT`, `401 BAD_TOKEN`, `409`, `404`, `500`). Secrets — provider API keys, licence keys,
client keys, JWTs, bcrypt hashes, internal service tokens — are redacted, hostnames are replaced with
example equivalents, and long arrays are trimmed for readability. Response *shapes* are unmodified.

## Destructive endpoints

A handful of requests are marked 🚨 in their description and were **deliberately not executed** when
these examples were captured, because they destroy platform-wide state with no undo:

- `GET /api/v1/user/log-out-all-users` — force-logout of every user
- `POST /api/v1/scan/clear-indexing` — wipes indexed metadata, permissions, shares and embeddings
- `DELETE /api/v1/genai/qa-logs`, `DELETE /api/v1/mcp/chat-logs` — purge all chat history
- `POST /api/v1/app-updates/execute` — full platform update with downtime
- `POST /api/v1/license/add-settings`, `POST /api/v1/genai/model-providers/save-providers`,
  `POST /api/v1/genai/mcp-server-settings`, `POST /api/v1/genai/switch-embedding-model`

Their request bodies are still accurate and ready to send — read the description before you do.

Deletes are **hard deletes**: referential integrity is enforced by PostgreSQL triggers and there are no
soft-delete columns anywhere in the schema.

item.name: 00 · Authentication & Session
item.description:
Log in as the super admin and manage the session. **Run `Login as Super Admin` first** — its test script stores `adminToken` / `refreshToken` as collection variables, and every other request in this collection inherits `Authorization: Bearer {{adminToken}}` from collection-level auth.

`entrance/*` and `token/*` bypass the `is-logged-in` policy, so those requests are marked *No Auth*.

request.description:
Exchange the client API key + admin credentials for an access/refresh token pair.

`apiKey` is the **client key** of an active App Client (see *10 · App Clients*). The web app reads it from `/assets/data/config/appConfig.json`.

If 2FA is enabled (`ssoSettings.twoFactorAuthConfig`), the response carries `otpEnabled: true` and no usable token until you complete `PUT /api/v1/entrance/verify-otp`.
Public. Returns the SSO / 2FA configuration the login screen must render (`authProvider`, `ssoEnabled`, OTP channel). Mirrors what *11 · App Settings → SSO & 2FA* writes.
The authenticated principal behind `{{adminToken}}`. Check `isSuperAdmin: true` here before assuming the rest of this collection will pass the `is-super-admin` policy.

response.name: 200 OK
response.body:
{
  "user": {
    "id": 1,
    "email": "admin@your-company.com",
    "emailStatus": "confirmed",
    "mobile": null,
    "mobileStatus": "confirmed",
    "name": "Admin",
    "isSuperAdmin": true,
    "isAdUser": false,
    "isGuestUser": false,
    "isAnonymousUser": false,
    "sid": null,
    "otpExpiresAt": "1567076023000",

}

response.name: 401 Unauthorized — bad/expired token

request.description:
Send a fresh OTP to the logged-in user over the configured channel. Unlike the rest of `entrance/*`, this one **requires** a valid token (`entrance/send-otp: is-logged-in`).

`otpSent: false` means 2FA is currently disabled — nothing was sent.
Second leg of the 2FA login. `rememberToken` comes from the login response when `otpEnabled: true`. Returns the same token bundle as a plain login.
Re-send the login OTP for an in-flight 2FA attempt, keyed by the `rememberToken` issued at login. Public — no token exists yet at this point in the flow.

Okta/SSO callback verification — validates an externally issued JWT against the client key and mints an Olympus session. Fails with `OKTA_LOGIN_CHANNEL_DISABLED` unless `ssoSettings.authProvider` is set to `okta`.

response.name: 400 Bad Request — SSO not enabled

request.description: Mail a password-reset link. Always answers 200 with the same message whether or not the account exists (no user enumeration). Requires working SMTP — see *07 · Email Settings*.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description: Complete the reset: exchange the emailed `passwordResetToken` for a new password.
Swap a refresh token for a fresh access token. Access tokens are short-lived (5 h); the refresh token may be rotated, so always persist both halves of the response.

request.description: Revoke a single access token immediately (Redis blacklist) instead of waiting for it to expire. **Blacklisting `{{adminToken}}` ends your own session** — re-run the login request afterwards.

request.description: Blacklist every outstanding token for one user — the self-service version of `POST /api/v1/user/log-out-from-all-apps`. Policy is `true` (unauthenticated) because it is also called from the logout path after the token is already gone.
Ends the current session. Returns plain `OK`, not JSON.

Update **your own** admin profile (name / mobile / password) — this is the *My Profile* form, scoped to `req.me`, so it is `is-logged-in` rather than `is-super-admin`. Send `password` only when actually changing it.

item.name: 01 · Admin Dashboard
item.description: Read-only aggregates behind the admin landing page. Every action is `dashboard/*: is-super-admin`. None of them take inputs.

request.description: Top KPI cards: total users, files, folders and bytes stored across all mount points.
User breakdown by type (local / AD-LDAP / guest / anonymous) for the users chart.
Sessions grouped by client platform (Web / Windows / Mac / Android / iOS), derived from the `platform` column on the audit log.
Counts of file/folder operations (upload, download, delete, share, …) over the reporting window.
Returns `true` while the super admin still uses the shipped default password. The UI shows a persistent security banner until this flips to `false`.

item.name: 02 · User Management
item.description:
Full user lifecycle. `user/*` is `is-super-admin` except `verify-and-update` (public, e-mail proof token) and `update-profile` (self-service — kept in *00 · Authentication*).

**Requests are ordered as a runnable lifecycle:** create → read → toggle flags → resend invite → force logout → delete. `Create User` saves `{{testUserId}}`, which every later request consumes.

⚠️ `user/delete` is a **hard delete** — Postgres triggers cascade to permissions, shares, favourites, comments and embeddings. There is no soft-delete column and no undo.

request.description:
Create a local (non-AD) user. The account is created already `confirmed`; a welcome/confirmation mail is sent when SMTP is configured.

Set `isGuestUser: true` to have the *Guest User Policies* (storage cap, auto-delete) apply and to land the user on the guest home mount.

request.description:
Allow/deny this user to serve uploaded HTML as a browsable page. Community-tier feature — super admin only, no licence gate.

Hosted content is deliberately served from the **`mobile-api.*` origin, never the app origin**, so a stored-XSS payload cannot read another user's token out of `localStorage`.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description: Promote a guest to a full user, or demote a full user to guest. Flipping to guest subjects the account to the storage quota and auto-delete window from *11 · App Settings → Guest User Policies*.
Re-issue the account confirmation e-mail. Silently succeeds (`data: {}`) even when the address is already confirmed. Requires working SMTP.
Blacklist every access token issued to one user across every device. `expiredTokens` lists what was revoked — empty when the user has no live sessions.

🚨 **Platform-wide force logout.** Blacklists every token for every user and broadcasts a `LOGOUT_EVENT` socket signal. Intended for incident response / before a maintenance window.

Not executed while building this collection — it would have kicked every live session on the reference server.

Permanently destroy the user. The response's `cascade` block reports exactly what the Postgres triggers removed alongside the row: owned files/directories, embeddings, comments, `EntityPermission` grants (both as subtree owner and as grantee), and `SharedItems` rows.

Deleting an id that no longer exists answers **500 `UNKNOWN_SERVER_ERROR`**, not 404.

request.description: Public onboarding completion — the invited user exchanges the `emailProofToken` from their invitation mail for a confirmed account with a name and password. Policy is `true`; the token is the only credential.

item.name: 03 · Mount Points
item.description:
SMB / NFS shares Olympus mounts and indexes. `mount-settings/*` is `is-super-admin`, except `mount-points` and `get-mount-status`, which drop to `is-logged-in` so the user panel can render drive names.

**Lifecycle order:** add → read → status → update → unmount → delete. `Add Mount Point` saves `{{mountId}}`.

`accessControlType` is the single most consequential field — it decides how filesystem ACLs map to Olympus permissions (`AD_ACLS`, `FULL_RW`, `READ_ONLY`, `FULL_RW_TO_AD_USERS`, `CREATOR_ADMIN_OTHERS_NULL`). Changing it on a populated mount re-derives permissions on the next scan.

request.description:
Register and mount a share. The server performs the actual `mount` syscall unless `enabled: false`, so a bad host/credential pair fails here rather than silently later.

**Validation trap:** with `accessControlType: CREATOR_ADMIN_OTHERS_NULL` on an NFS mount you must supply at least one `mountPointAdminUserIds` entry, otherwise you get `400 BAD_INPUT`.

`isUsersHome: true` marks the mount as the per-user home root — only one mount may hold it, and `applicableUserTypes` then decides which user types are seeded there.

body.raw:
{
  "enabled": false,
  "mountType": "nfs",
  "mountPath": "nfs-server-1.internal:/postman-qa",
  "username": "",
  "password": "",
  "accessControlType": "CREATOR_ADMIN_OTHERS_NULL",
  "isSharingEnabled": true,
  "sharingLevel": "BOTH",
  "allowAnonymousSharing": false,
  "nfsVersion": 4,
  "smbVersion": 3,
  "mountOptions": "nolock,soft",
  "options": {},
  "defaultSharingExpiryPeriod": 0,
  "enableRealTimeFilePermissionCheck": true,
  "enableFileOwnershipSync": false,
  "isUsersHome": false,
  "applicableUserTypes": [],
  "maxFileSizeBytes": 10485760,
  "isGenAiEnabled": true,
  "mountPointAdminUserIds": [
    1
  ]
}

item.name: 04 · Scan & Indexing
item.description:
Filesystem scanning keeps the `File` / `Directory` tables in sync with what is actually on the shares. `scan/*` is `is-super-admin` except `resync-directory`, which is `is-logged-in`.

Scanning normally runs from the **`scan` cron job**, whose cadence is admin-controlled in *Admin → Cron Job Settings* — these endpoints are the manual override.

request.description:
Kick off a scan and return immediately — the work happens on the RabbitMQ scan pipeline.

Omit `mountId` to scan **every enabled mount** sequentially (mounts are never scanned in parallel: concurrent `mount` syscalls race). With `mountId` set, only that mount runs and you get `404 MOUNT_NOT_FOUND` if it is missing or disabled.

Watch progress live via `subscribe-scan-events` below.

Re-scan one folder subtree instead of a whole mount — the *Refresh* action in the file browser. Children are processed asynchronously.

The only `is-logged-in` action in `scan/*`, because ordinary users trigger it.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description:
🚨 **Destructive.** Wipes the indexed metadata for a mount (or all mounts when `mountId` is omitted): `File` / `Directory` rows and everything cascading off them — permissions, shares, favourites, comments, embeddings. Files on the share are not touched, but the next scan rebuilds from zero and every embedding must be regenerated.

Deliberately **not executed** while building this collection.

**Socket.io request, not plain HTTP** — issue it through `sails.io.js` / a Socket.IO client, not the Postman HTTP runner.

Joins the `scan-updates` room and starts receiving `scanUpdate` events. Deliberately room-scoped and super-admin gated: scan log lines contain internal mount paths and host addresses, so they must never be blasted to every connected socket.
Leaves the `scan-updates` room. Socket.io request — see the note on subscribe.

item.name: 05 · Cron Jobs
item.description:
Scheduled platform jobs. `cron-job/*` is `is-super-admin`.

Schedules live in the `CronJob` table, **not** in code — this admin surface is the source of truth and the workers hot-reload changes within ~5 minutes (or immediately via `reload`).

`scope` decides where a job runs (`worker` = the dedicated worker process only, `all` = every Sails process); `executionMode: single` adds a Redis lock so only one instance fires per tick.

Shipped jobs: `scan`, `cleanup`, `mount_recovery`, `license-sync`, `reembed-publisher`, `guest-auto-delete`.

request.description: All jobs with their cron expression, enabled flag, scope, and last-run outcome (`lastRun`, `isLastRunSuccess`, `isRunning`).

item.name: 07 · Email Settings
item.description:
Outbound SMTP/SES configuration. All three actions are explicitly `is-super-admin`.

Without working mail, user invitations, confirmation mails and password recovery all fail silently from the end user's perspective — validate with `test-settings` after every change.

request.description:
Current mail configuration: the `transporters` array, which one is active (`useTransporter`), the `from` address, and `alwaysSendTo` (a catch-all override useful in staging).

`testMode: true` logs mail instead of delivering it.

Replace the mail configuration. `transporters` is a full array — send every transporter you want to keep, not a delta.

Set `requiresAuth: false` and leave `auth` blank for an unauthenticated relay (the reference server uses MailHog that way). For SES, set `service: "SES"` and populate `region`.

Send a real test message using the **posted** settings without saving them — validate first, then persist with `update-settings`. `to` is the recipient of the test mail.

The response carries the transport's `messageId` plus `accepted` / `rejected` recipient lists.

response.name: 200 OK

item.name: 08 · AD / LDAP Integration
item.description:
Active Directory / LDAP directory binding. `ad-ldap/*` is `is-super-admin` — the payload carries the directory **bind credentials**, so neither reads nor writes are open to ordinary users.

**At most one settings row may exist.** `add-settings` answers `409` if one is already there — update it or delete it first. Requests below run as a full add → read → update → test → delete cycle.

`serviceType`: `1` = Active Directory, `2` = generic LDAP. Flipping `ldapOn` to `true` changes how `entrance/login` resolves accounts, so validate with `test-settings` before enabling it.

request.description:
Create the (single) directory configuration. Note this action **stores without connecting** — it does not validate reachability, so run `test-settings` separately.

`addEveryUserToDomainUsersGroup: true` auto-joins every synced account to *Domain Users*, which is what most `AD_ACLS` mounts expect.
The stored configuration — **including `ldapPassword` in clear text**, which is why this read is super-admin gated. Returns `data: {}` when no directory is configured.
Partial update — only `id` is required; omitted fields keep their stored value.

Attempt a bind with the **posted** credentials without saving them. Returns `400 CONNECTION_FAILED` when the host is unreachable or the bind DN/password is wrong — run this before setting `ldapOn: true`.

response.name: 400 Bad Request — host unreachable

request.description: Remove the directory binding and fall back to local authentication. Already-synced AD user accounts survive but can no longer authenticate against the directory.

item.name: 09 · License
item.description:
Licence activation and status. `license/*` is `is-super-admin` — `get-settings` returns the licence key itself.

The licence drives feature gating platform-wide: Enterprise unlocks watermarking, custom system prompts, the Telegram bridge and Range-aware fast downloads. Trial defaults are **90 days / 20 users**.

The `license-sync` cron job (*05 · Cron Jobs*) re-validates against the licence server hourly; `licenseSyncStatus` reports how close the install is to a stale-sync lock.

request.description: Full licence state: type/status, bound e-mail and domain, the key, seat and storage caps (`maxUsers`, `maxStorage`), current consumption, expiry, and the sync-health block (`isStale`, `daysSinceLastSync`, `daysUntilLock`).

Activate or replace the licence. `licenseDomain` must match the host the platform is served on — a mismatch fails activation.

⚠️ Not executed against the reference server: it would have overwritten a live licence.

Ask the licence server to release this key from its currently bound device so it can be activated on new hardware — the supported path when rebuilding or moving a server.

⚠️ Not executed: it mutates state on the external licence service.

item.name: 10 · App Clients
item.description:
The API keys each front-end presents as `apiKey` at login. `app-clients/*` is `is-super-admin`.

Six clients ship by default (Web, Windows, Mac, Android, iOS, Bots). Disabling one immediately blocks **new logins** from that platform; existing tokens keep working until they expire, so pair a toggle with `user/log-out-all-users` if you need an instant cutoff.

Clients cannot be created or deleted through the API — only toggled.

request.description:
All clients with their `clientKey`. The Web client's key is the `apiKey` the browser app reads from `appConfig.json`; the `Bot` client's key is what the bot-login exchange uses.

Client keys are credentials — the sample response has them redacted.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description: Enable/disable one client. **Do not disable the Web client (id 1)** unless you intend to lock everyone out of the browser UI, including this admin panel.

item.name: 11 · App Settings
item.description: Platform-wide settings stored in the `AppSettings` table, one sub-folder per settings group. All groups are `is-super-admin`, with two deliberate exceptions noted inline.

item.name: SSO & Two-Factor
item.description: `app-settings/sns/*` — the authentication provider and 2FA channel. The public `entrance/get-auth-modes` endpoint reads the same record so the login screen can render the right form.

request.description: Current `authProvider` (`otp` / `okta`), `ssoEnabled` flag, the Okta config block, and `twoFactorAuthConfig.otpChannel` (`EMAIL` / `SMS`).

Replace the auth configuration. Setting `authProvider: "okta"` + `ssoEnabled: true` activates the `entrance/logged-in` SSO callback; leave `oktaConfig` out entirely when staying on OTP.

⚠️ A bad Okta config can lock everyone out of login — verify in a private window before closing your admin session.

item.name: Splash Screen
item.description:
`app-settings/splash-screen/*` — the post-login announcement dialog.

Write is super-admin; **read is deliberately `is-logged-in`** because every user's layout bootstrap fetches it to decide whether to show the dialog.

request.description:
Current splash content. `splashScreenContent` is rich HTML and may embed base64 images, so this response can be large (~15 KB on the reference server — truncated in the sample below).

Clients remember the last `splashScreenVersion` a user dismissed; bumping the number re-shows the dialog to everyone.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description: Replace the splash content. **Increment `splashScreenVersion`** to force the dialog back on for users who already dismissed the previous one.

item.name: Guest User Policies
item.description: `app-settings/guest-user-policies/*` — quota and lifecycle rules applied to accounts flagged `isGuestUser`. Super-admin for both read and write; there is no user-facing read path.

request.description: Storage cap, e-mail auto-confirm behaviour, whether guest files are destroyed with the account, and the auto-delete window.

Replace the guest policy.

- `maxStorageBytes` — per-guest quota (1073741824 = 1 GiB)
- `destroyFilesOnGuestDelete` — hard-delete the guest's files with the account
- `autoConfirmEmail` — skip the confirmation mail for self-registered guests
- `autoDelete` — `{ enabled, afterDays, afterHours }`, enforced by the `guest-auto-delete` cron job

item.name: User Home Seeds
item.description: `app-settings/user-home-seeds/*` — files copied into every new user's home folder on first login. Requires a mount flagged `isUsersHome: true` (see *03 · Mount Points*).

request.description: The active home mount, the `maxFiles` cap, and the current seed files with their uuid `id`, size, mimetype and `applicableUserTypes` (empty = all user types).

Upload a new seed file. **`multipart/form-data`, field name `file`** — let Postman set the boundary; do not add a `Content-Type` header by hand.

Rejected once the seed count reaches `maxFiles`.
Update one seed entry, addressed by the uuid `id` from `get-settings` — used to narrow a seed to specific `applicableUserTypes`.

Remove a seed file so it stops being copied into new home folders. Copies already placed in existing users' homes are left alone.

The action declares no inputs — the seed `id` is read straight off the request body/query by the handler.

item.name: Watermark
item.description:
`app-settings/watermark/*` — the overlay stamped on previewed/downloaded documents.

Read is `is-super-admin`; **write additionally requires an Enterprise licence** (`['is-super-admin', 'is-enterprise']`), so `update-settings` answers a licence error on Community/Trial installs.

request.description: Current watermark configuration.

Configure the overlay. `drawingOption` picks the diagonal layout: `dia1`, `dia2` or `both`. `addTimestamp` / `addUserEmail` append per-download provenance to `watermarkText`, which is what makes a leaked document traceable.

**Enterprise licence required.**

item.name: 12 · Sharing Administration
item.description:
Platform-wide visibility into every share any user has created, plus the ability to revoke them. These five `share/*` actions are `is-super-admin`; the rest of the sharing surface is per-resource and lives in the *User APIs* collection.

The listing endpoints are **POST** because the filter payload is a JSON body, not a query string.

request.description: Aggregate counters for files and directories: total, new in window, private vs public.

Every shared file on the platform, across all owners.

`filter` is a free-form object (e.g. `{"shareType": "public"}`); `startDate` / `endDate` are epoch ms. Each row carries the `SharedItems` id you pass to the delete endpoint below.
Directory equivalent of the above.
Same filters, returned as **CSV** (`Id, File, File Path, Mount Point, Shared By, Shared To, Share Type, Shared As, Shared At`). Use *Send and Download*.

response.name: 200 OK — header row only — no shares
response.body: "Id","File","File Path","Mount Point","Shared By","Shared To","Share Type","Shared As","Shared At"

request.description: Directory equivalent of the CSV export.
Revoke one file share by its `SharedItems` id — works on any user's share, which is the point of the admin variant. Public links stop resolving immediately.
Revoke one directory share by its `SharedItems` id. Recipients lose access to the whole shared subtree.

item.name: 13 · GenAI Administration
item.description:
The whole `genai/*` controller — provider keys, model selection, embedding administration, prompts and job monitoring. **Every action is `is-super-admin`, reads included**, because these endpoints expose provider API keys and self-hosted Ollama URLs, run provider calls with stored credentials, return other users' chat history, and rewrite the platform's default LLM configuration.

Do not confuse this with the user-facing `gen-ai/*` controller (document chat), which is `is-logged-in` and lives in the *User APIs* collection. The two differ by a single hyphen.

Most of these actions proxy through the Sails API to the Flask GenAI service, so a `500` here often means the downstream service or a third-party provider is unreachable rather than a bad request — the proxied error text is passed through verbatim.

item.name: Dashboard & Jobs
item.description: Health and throughput of the GenAI pipeline (Flask API + RabbitMQ worker + Milvus).

request.description: Aggregate pipeline counters — embedded files, queued/failed jobs, vector counts.
Per-dependency health of the GenAI stack (Flask, worker, vector store, queue). The first thing to check when embeddings stop appearing.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description:
Embedding / summary job queue with progress and status.

`job_data` echoes the payload handed to the worker, including the internal download URL and its `X-Olympus-Internal-Token` header — redacted in the sample response, and a good reason to keep this endpoint admin-only.

Filter with `status` (`queued` / `processing` / `completed` / `failed`) and `jobType` (`add_embedding`, `summary`, …).

Re-publish a job to RabbitMQ after fixing whatever made it fail.

**Only `failed` jobs may be re-queued** — anything else answers `400` with the current status; an unknown `jobId` answers `404`.

response.name: 400 Bad Request — job already completed

item.name: Q&A Logs
item.description: Every question asked of the document-chat pipeline, platform-wide.

request.description: Paginated Q&A history: question, full answer, model used, latency, and the source entity. This returns **all users'** conversations, which is why it is super-admin gated.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description:
🚨 **Purges the entire Q&A log table** — there is no date filter and no undo. A data-retention tool, not a maintenance one.

Deliberately not executed while building this collection.

item.name: AI Model Setup
item.description:
Which model handles which capability. `genai/ai-model-setup/*` is super-admin.

Models are identified as `provider:name` (e.g. `chatgpt:gpt-5`, `gemini:models/gemini-embedding-2`).

request.description: The platform defaults for each capability — `embedding`, `summary`, `chat`, `vision` — plus the active `chunkSettings`.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description:
Set the default model per capability. Send only the capabilities you are changing.

⚠️ Changing `embedding` here does **not** migrate existing vectors — use *Embedding Administration → switch-embedding-model*, which queues the re-embed.
Every model selected across all enabled providers, optionally narrowed by capability. Feeds the model pickers in the admin UI.

item.name: Model Providers
item.description:
Credentials and model selection per LLM provider. `genai/model-providers/*` is super-admin — the stored document contains **live API keys**.

Two families: hosted providers under the `public` block (`chatgpt`, `claude`, `gemini`, `deepseek`, `kimi`, `openrouter`) and self-hosted Ollama under `self`.

⚠️ `save-providers` writes the whole `public` block — the server read-merges so an unrelated provider is not wiped, but always fetch `saved-providers` first and post back a complete picture.

request.description:
The full provider configuration document from CouchDB: every provider's `apiKey`, `enabled` flag, `validated` status and `selectedModels` list.

**API keys are returned in clear text** — the sample response has them redacted and its model lists trimmed (the live payload is ~23 KB).

response.name: 200 OK

request.description:
Validate a provider key **before** saving it. Pass `url` instead of `apiKey` for self-hosted endpoints.

Note the transport quirk: a rejected key still returns HTTP 200 with `success: false` and the proxied provider error in `message`.

response.name: 200 OK — invalid key

request.description:
Enumerate the models a key can reach, annotated with detected `capabilities` and a `popular` flag. This is what populates the selection checkboxes.

An invalid key yields an empty `models` array rather than an error.

Persist provider config. `public` holds hosted providers keyed by name; `self` holds the Ollama block.

⚠️ Not executed against the reference server — it would have overwritten live provider credentials.

Complete the OpenRouter OAuth **PKCE** flow: exchange the authorization `code` plus the `codeVerifier` held by the browser for a durable API key, which is then stored as `public.openrouter`.

OpenRouter acts as a gateway — it is also the first OpenAI-compatible provider wired up for embeddings, not just chat.
Browse the **remote Ollama catalogue** (models available to pull), filtered by `query` / `tag` and ordered by `sort`.
What is actually pulled onto the Ollama host right now.
Every tag/quantisation available for one catalogue model, with parameter counts and sizes — how you choose between e.g. `llama3.2:1b` and `llama3.2:3b`. (Sample trimmed; the live payload is ~8 KB.)
Whether a specific model is installed on the Ollama host, and the progress of an in-flight pull.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description:
Pull a model onto the self-hosted Ollama server. Long-running — poll `check-ollama-model-status` for progress.

⚠️ Known limitation: the pull has **no server-side timeout**, so a stalled download can wedge with no signal. Not executed here (multi-GB download).
Delete a pulled model from the Ollama host to reclaim disk. Any capability still pointing at it will start failing — reassign in *AI Model Setup* first.
Remove the self-hosted Ollama block (`self`) from the provider document. Pulled models stay on the Ollama host; Olympus just stops pointing at it.

item.name: Embedding Administration
item.description:
`genai/embedding-admin/*` — probing candidate embedding models, migrating between them, and the capability registry that bounds chunk sizes.

Every action is super-admin gated so a logged-in non-admin cannot probe arbitrary models using the platform's stored API keys.

**Typical migration:** `probe-embedding-model` → `embedding-model-impact` → `switch-embedding-model` → poll `reembed-status`.

request.description:
Embed a throwaway string with a candidate model to discover its real `dimension` and round-trip latency before committing. `apiKey` is optional — omit it and the key is resolved from the stored provider document.

For Ollama, pass the tag-qualified `ollamaFullName` (e.g. `nomic-embed-text:latest`).

query.description: Optional override; otherwise resolved from stored config

request.description: Dry-run a model switch: how many files and vectors would have to be re-embedded, and whether the dimension change forces a Milvus collection rebuild. Read this before `switch-embedding-model`.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description:
Change the active embedding model. With `reEmbedAll: true` (the default) every previously embedded file is queued for re-embedding — the `reembed-publisher` cron job drains the batch.

For Ollama include the tag in `newName`. Optionally pass fresh `chunkSettings` in the same call so chunking and model change land together.

⚠️ Not executed — it would have re-embedded the whole reference corpus.
Progress of the active re-embed batch — counts, current batch id, and whether a migration is still running.

Abort a running re-embed. Omit `batchId` to cancel the active batch. Files already processed keep their **new** vectors while the rest keep the old ones, so the corpus is left mixed — finish or restart the migration.

Rebuild the vector-store indices. With `reEmbedAll: true` the source text is re-embedded as well; with `false` only the index structures are rebuilt from existing vectors — the cheaper repair for a corrupted index.

The valid chunk-size window for a model, derived from its context length and the capability registry's `safetyHeadroom`. Omit `embeddingModel` for raw per-file-type defaults. The admin UI uses this to clamp the chunk-size slider.
The capability registry: `contextTokens` / `dimension` per known model, plus the `defaults` block used for models that are not registered.

Register or update one model's capabilities. `modelId` is the canonical `provider:name` id **and may itself contain colons** — the router takes the whole remainder of the path.

Use this when you add a model the registry does not know about yet, so chunk bounds stop falling back to the conservative defaults.
Drop a model from the registry. It falls back to the `defaults` block; existing vectors are untouched.

Tune the fallback used for unregistered models.

- `contextTokens` — assumed context window
- `charsPerToken` — rough chars/token ratio for size estimation
- `safetyHeadroom` — fraction of the context to actually use (0–1)

Lowering `safetyHeadroom` shrinks every derived chunk bound platform-wide.

item.name: System Prompts
item.description:
`genai/system-prompts/*` — the prompt templates driving intent classification, RAG answer generation and summarisation.

Reads are `is-super-admin`; **writes additionally require an Enterprise licence** (`save`, `reset`, `restore-version` are `['is-super-admin', 'is-enterprise']`).

Prompts are versioned: every `save` appends a version, so `restore-version` can roll back a regression. Shipped keys: `image_visual_analysis`, `intent_classification`, `qa_folder_summary`, `qa_inclusive`, `qa_open`, `qa_restrictive`, `qa_summary`, `summary_generation`.

request.description:
Overwrite a prompt. `content` replaces the template wholesale; `versionLabel` is a human note attached to the new version.

**Enterprise licence required.** Preserve the template's placeholders — the RAG chain interpolates context and question into them, and dropping one silently degrades answer quality.

⚠️ Not executed against the reference server.
Restore one prompt to its shipped default. **Enterprise licence required.**
Roll back to an earlier saved version. `versionIndex` is the 0-based position in the prompt's version array from `get-one`. **Enterprise licence required.**

item.name: MCP Server Settings
item.description: `genai/mcp-server/*` — which providers and models the MCP agent (the tool-calling brain behind folder chat, the Telegram bridge and external MCP clients) may use. Super-admin.

request.description: Current MCP agent configuration: enabled providers, the active chat provider/model, the active image provider/model, and the Sails callback URL the agent uses for tool calls.

Save the MCP agent configuration.

`enabledProviders` is the allow-list; `activeProviderModel` picks the reasoning model. Reasoning effort and max-output tokens are resolved automatically from the provider capability layer — you do not set them here.

⚠️ Not executed: it would have rewritten the live MCP configuration.
Models eligible for the MCP agent, filtered by capability — the option list behind the picker in the settings form.

item.name: Embedding Limits
item.description: Guard-rail on recursive folder embedding. Policy here is `gen-ai/*: is-logged-in`, but the only caller is the admin panel — included for completeness of the admin surface.

request.description: The current cap on how many files a single *embed folder recursively* action may enqueue.
Raise or lower the recursive-embed cap. This is the brake that stops one click on a huge folder from flooding the embedding queue.

item.name: 14 · MCP Chat Logs
item.description:
Admin view over MCP agent conversations. These two actions live in the `mcp/` controller alongside the user-facing chat actions, so they carry **individual** `is-super-admin` policies rather than a wildcard — `mcp/get-chat-logs` and `mcp/clear-chat-logs`.

Both expose or destroy **every** user's MCP conversation history.

request.description:
Paginated MCP messages across all sessions: session id, user, folder context, role and full message text — including the `🔧 Tool [...] called` traces that show which MCP tools the agent invoked.

`role` filters `user` vs `assistant`; `userId` / `directoryId` accept `0` to mean *no filter*.

response.name: 200 OK

request.description:
🚨 **Purges all MCP chat history** for every user and session. No filter, no undo.

Deliberately not executed while building this collection.

item.name: 15 · App Updates
item.description:
Container image update checks and the one-click updater. `app-updates/*` is `is-super-admin`.

These endpoints **proxy to the Olympus setup app** (the installer that runs on port 8888). If that container is not running — as on the reference server used to capture these examples — you get `"Setup app not reachable"` / `STATUS_CHECK_FAILED` rather than a Sails error. That is the expected response on installs where the setup app was stopped after provisioning.

request.description: Actively query the registry for newer image tags. Slower than `latest-check` because it makes live registry calls.
The **cached** result of the last update check — `has_updates`, `update_count`, the per-image list and `last_checked`. This is what the admin badge polls.

response.name: 200 OK — setup app stopped

request.description:
🚨 **Starts the platform update**: pulls new images and recreates containers, which means downtime for every service. Poll `status` for progress.

Note the updater re-syncs the static config files (compose files, postgres/redis confs) but **not** generated ones (`.env`, `production.js`, `appConfig.json`, `init.sql`) — schema changes arrive through Knex migrations instead.

Obviously not executed while building this collection.
Progress of an in-flight update. Poll this after `execute`.
